metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for Barrett's surveillance EGD; periprocedural anticoagulation review

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: MedicationRequest warfarin sodium 5 mg = active; referral lists warfarin as current anticoagulant
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: hematology transitioned patient off warfarin to apixaban 5 mg twice daily approximately 4 months ago (~March 2026); patient no longer takes warfarin
planted_discrepancies.note: narrative-vs-pharmacy anticoagulant conflict; periprocedural hold differs materially (warfarin ~5-day hold with INR normalization vs apixaban ~1-2 day hold, no bridging). Prior hematology note documents the switch but the active med list was never reconciled.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: INR 2.4 presented in referral as the anticoagulation status
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: INR was drawn 2026-01-15 while still on warfarin, before the switch to apixaban; INR is not a meaningful monitoring parameter on a DOAC
planted_discrepancies.note: the INR is stale and gives a false picture of current anticoagulation; do not use it to guide the periprocedural plan

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: the 2020 DVT occurred within weeks of right knee arthroscopy — provoked by surgery/immobilization
planted_discrepancies.note: provoked vs unprovoked changes the long-term thrombotic risk rationale; consistent with hematology's low-thrombotic-risk categorization and their decision that no bridge is needed

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient refers to OTC fish oil as 'my other blood thinner'; fish oil is not an anticoagulant
planted_discrepancies.note: 'blood thinner' = fish oil trap; clarify this is a supplement, not therapeutic anticoagulation; no periprocedural hold required for it

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms taking it every morning for Barrett's/reflux

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: Barrett's esophagus, surveillance due; C2M4, no dysplasia history
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms he is asymptomatic — no heartburn, no dysphagia, no weight loss — here purely for scheduled surveillance

medication_labels: omeprazole 40 MG Delayed Release Oral Capsule

prior_notes.text: Surveillance esophagogastroduodenoscopy. Salmon-colored mucosa extending in a tongue-like fashion in the distal esophagus, consistent with Barrett's esophagus, Prague classification C2M4. Four-quadrant biopsies obtained every 2 cm per Seattle protocol; targeted biopsies of any mucosal irregularity — none seen. Stomach and duodenum normal. Impression: Barrett's esophagus C2M4. Await histology; if no dysplasia, surveillance EGD in ~2 years.

Follow-up for history of lower-extremity DVT (2020, provoked — occurred following right knee arthroscopy with post-operative immobilization). Patient has remained on long-term anticoagulation. Given provoked etiology and no recurrence, categorized as LOW thrombotic risk. Transitioning from warfarin to apixaban 5 mg PO twice daily for ease of monitoring; warfarin discontinued today, apixaban started after last therapeutic INR. No bridging indicated for future low-bleeding-risk procedures. For any upcoming endoscopy, hold apixaban per procedural bleeding risk; bridging is NOT required.

code.text: Deep vein thrombosis (disorder) — charted as unprovoked, 2020

Condition._discrepancy: D3: chart labels DVT unprovoked; patient reports it followed right knee arthroscopy (provoked)

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Esophageal biopsies: intestinal metaplasia consistent with Barrett's esophagus. Negative for dysplasia. No malignancy.

transcript:
DR: Morning, Mr. Ostrowski. You're in today so we can plan the upper endoscopy — the Barrett's surveillance scope. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me get your vitals down — you're 5 foot 10, 183 pounds. Blood pressure's 126 over 78, heart rate 72, temp 98.1, breathing 14, and oxygen's 98 percent on room air.
PT: Fine, honestly. No heartburn, no trouble swallowing, weight's steady. I feel like I'm only here because it's on the calendar.
DR: That's exactly right, and that's good news — this is routine surveillance. Your last scope showed the Barrett's, C2 M4, and the biopsies had no dysplasia, so we just keep an eye on it. You're still on the omeprazole?
PT: Every morning, forty milligrams. Never miss it.
DR: Perfect. Now the main thing I need to sort out is the blood thinner. The chart has you down for warfarin.
PT: Oh — no, they took me off the warfarin. Hematology switched me over to apixaban back in the spring, March I think. Twice a day now. Much easier, no more finger-stick checks.
DR: That's an important correction — so no warfarin, you're on apixaban. And your chart still has an INR from January, when you were on the warfarin. That number doesn't really apply anymore.
PT: Right, they don't check that number for me now.
DR: Understood. And remind me about the clot back in 2020 — was there a reason it happened?
PT: Yeah, it was after my knee surgery. The arthroscopy on the right knee. I was laid up for a couple weeks and then the leg swelled up.
DR: Okay — so it was provoked, related to the surgery. Hematology has you as low-risk, and their note says no bridging is needed, which fits. Any other medicines or supplements?
PT: Just a fish oil, my other blood thinner I guess. Over the counter.
DR: Good to know, but fish oil isn't an actual anticoagulant, so we don't hold it for procedures. I'll get the apixaban hold instructions squared away and the team will send your prep.
PT: Sounds good.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Ostrowski is a 64-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan surveillance EGD for Barrett's esophagus (C2M4, no dysplasia on 2024 biopsies; surveillance due). He is asymptomatic — denies heartburn, dysphagia, odynophagia, and weight loss. He confirms daily omeprazole 40 mg. Regarding anticoagulation: chart and referral list warfarin, but the patient reports hematology transitioned him from warfarin to apixaban 5 mg twice daily approximately 4 months ago (~March 2026); he no longer takes warfarin. He clarifies his 2020 lower-extremity DVT was provoked, occurring after right knee arthroscopy with post-operative immobilization (charted as unprovoked). He also takes OTC fish oil, which he colloquially calls a 'blood thinner'; this is a supplement, not an anticoagulant. No drug allergies reported.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 126/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 83 kg (183 lb), BMI 26.2. Labs — INR 2.4 (2026-01-15, drawn while on warfarin, prior to DOAC switch; not a current monitoring parameter). Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL and platelets 244 x10^3/uL (2026-06-30), both normal. No active anemia. Patient appears well.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Barrett's esophagus (C2M4, no dysplasia) — proceed with scheduled surveillance EGD with Seattle-protocol biopsies. Asymptomatic; low bleeding-risk procedure per ASGE.
- Anticoagulation reconciliation (HIGH PRIORITY): patient is on APIXABAN 5 mg BID, not warfarin — update active med list (discontinue warfarin, add apixaban). Periprocedural plan will be based on apixaban, not warfarin/INR. Hold apixaban per procedural protocol (typically 1-2 days pre-procedure for low-bleeding-risk endoscopy); the January INR is stale and will not be used.
- Thrombotic risk: 2020 DVT was PROVOKED (post-knee arthroscopy), not unprovoked — update problem list. Consistent with hematology's low-risk categorization; no bridging required per hematology note (2026-03-10).
- Fish oil is an OTC supplement, not an anticoagulant — no periprocedural hold needed; documented for completeness.
- GERD/Barrett's — continue omeprazole 40 mg daily.
- Scheduling + bowel/upper-endoscopy prep and anticoagulant hold instructions to follow from the team.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your scheduled upper endoscopy (EGD) to check your Barrett's esophagus
• You are feeling well with no heartburn or swallowing problems
• Your blood thinner and how to manage it around the procedure
• Your reflux medicine

Next steps
• We will schedule your upper endoscopy; the team will send prep instructions.
• Important: your record showed warfarin, but you are now on apixaban. We updated this. You will get specific instructions on when to stop the apixaban before the procedure. Please do not use the old warfarin/INR plan.
• Bridging (extra injections) is not needed — your clot in 2020 was related to your knee surgery and hematology considers you low risk.
• Fish oil is a supplement, not a true blood thinner, so it does not need to be stopped.
• Keep taking omeprazole each morning.
• Call us with trouble swallowing, vomiting blood, black stools, or new chest pain.
